name: Philodendron spiritus-sancti

morphology.leaf_shape: Narrowly elliptic to lanceolate, long and strap-like with an acuminate apex; distinctly coriaceous (leathery) leaf blade
morphology.leaf_ratio: Extremely elongated, approximately 8:1 to 12:1 length-to-width ratio (leaves 60–90 cm long × 5–10 cm wide)
morphology.texture: Leathery (coriaceous), thick and stiff in texture, glabrous on both surfaces with a subtle glossy sheen adaxially
morphology.petiole: Long cylindrical petioles, terete in cross-section, proportionally long relative to the leaf blade (40–60 cm), pale green with faint longitudinal striations
morphology.growth_habit: Hemiepiphytic scandent (climbing) habit; stems elongate, rooting along internodes; can climb several meters on tree trunks
morphology.mature_size: Overall plant height 2–4 m in natural habitat; leaves 60–90 cm long, petioles 40–60 cm; stems 2–3 cm in diameter

critical_traits: Extremely long, narrow strap-like leaves with an acuminate tip — the most defining trait
Leathery, coriaceous leaf texture — not thin or papery
Long, terete petioles that are proportionally long relative to the blade
Glabrous leaves with subtle adaxial gloss (no pubescence)
Lanceolate leaf shape — not cordate, not sagittate, not hastate
negative_traits: Short, squat leaves with low aspect ratio
Red or coloured abaxial leaf surfaces
habitat: Tropical moist lowland and montane forest; grows as a hemiepiphyte climbing on tree trunks in shaded understory with high humidity; well-drained organic-rich substrates; elevations from 200–800 m
origin: Espírito Santo, Brazil (critically endangered in its native range; restricted to a very small area of remaining Atlantic Forest)
